titleStmt.title: Letter from Maria Edgeworth to Frances Anne Edgeworth, May 19, 1819
titleStmt.author: Maria Edgeworth
titleStmt.sponsor: Wake Forest University, University of Tennessee, Xavier University, Texas A&M University
titleStmt.principal: Susan Egenolf, Meredith Hale, Hilary Havens, Jessica Richard, and Robin Runia

editionStmt.edition: First digital edition in TEI, date: January 14, 2026

availability.p: Reproduced by courtesy of Bodleian Library
availability.licence: Distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0) Unported License

msDesc.head:
Letter from
Maria Edgeworth 1768-1849 71477273 62567029
to Frances Anne Edgeworth,

editorialDecl.p: Edgeworth’s spelling and punctuation are retained in transcription, including non-standard spelling, line breaks, and hyphenation of words. The long s and ligatured forms are silently emended.

div.p:
Byrkley Lodge [LibNote]208[/LibNote] May 19.[superscript]h[/superscript] 1819 How melancholy how dreadful my dearest mother is the account of all you have gone through this last fortnight. Will it be any com. fort to you or will it add to your pain to know how much I pity you and how excessively anxious we are and must be about you & -about you all. I have no doubt that every possible prudent pre- caution has been taken by you & dear Lovell but - it is a most horrible situation & poor Lucy! What accumulated evils poured upon you all at once & just at the moment when we flattered ourselves that you were all most happy enjoying Lovells well de- served praises & gallant Sheriff's show.[insertion] I have rec.[superscript]d[/superscript] yours of the 7.t[superscript]h[/superscript] the 13.[superscript]th[/superscript] &the 9.[superscript]th[/superscript] - in [underline]this order[/underline]. [/insertion] One letter of yours, the first you wrote giving an account (I suppose) of poor Mary Anne Fox's danger has never yet reached us - I conclude it came to Lord Carringtons
the day we left him & that he forwarded it as he says he forwarded all letters for us to Knutsford. We shall find it there on our arrival. We leave this place tomorrow morning 11 o clock - shall be at M[superscript]r.[/superscript] & M[superscript]rs.[/superscript] Wedgwoods to dinner. They no longer live at Etruria they have just changed to Maer [deletion]near[/deletion] about 8 or 9 miles from Woore their post town. We shall stay with them till saturday . drink tea on saturday with M[superscript]r[/superscript]. & M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. Holland and spend 4 days with them & 4 with M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. M Proys - Then pursue our journey to Holyhead which we hope to reach by 29[superscript]th[/superscript]. or [underline]30[superscript]th[/superscript][/underline]. - All this I have told you I believe before but I repeat to make sure of there being no mistakes on my part. I [insertion] will write to Mrs Waller from Knutsford.[/insertion] think of going round by Black Castle in my way home to spend one day with my dear aunt; because from the time I arrive at home I shall never stir or think of seeing her till I have completed my manu- script & completed the correction of the proof sheets which cannot be till Xmass. One day would settle her mind & mine & I hope you will ap-
prove of this but I shall not mention [LibNote] 209 [/LibNote] it to my aunt till I have your answer dearest [underline]Mother [/underline]& I entreat you will say exactly what you wish God knows I wish I was with you this moment & regret heartily the cursed engagements which will de lay us this fortnight. I made only these Knutsford engagements when I first came to England & they have hampered us ever since. When shall I learn sense enough never to make any promises - ? And now dear mother what will you think about Honora's determination to stay with her aunt. I thought I was quite sure that you would approve of it and I) consider it as I do the very best thing on every account that c.[superscript]d[/superscript] be done: but one line in the last letter of [insertion]yours of[/insertion] of the 9[superscript]th[/superscript]. which I did not receive till yesterday evening, throws me in to anxiety. You say you have a [underline]latent[/underline] [underline]fear [deletion]now[/deletion] of dear Honora's offering[/underline] [underline]to stay with her aunts".[/underline] Now it had all been settled before your letter came & I never have shewn this line to any
one but Fanny because [insertion]as[/insertion] she agrees with me in thinking it would only give pain & could [insertion]not[/insertion] now alter any thing - - My aunts were very unwilling to let Honora stay urging that it would be very dull for her & too great a sacri fice &c but she urged it so much & I added in private converse with them that I dreaded much the effect [underline]upon Honora's mind of returning to[/underline] [underline]Edgeworth's Town[/underline]. Fanny joined me in this fear & told me that you had the same I hope my dearest mother I have not done wrong for I certainly did use my utmost [deletion]power[/deletion] influence to pre- vail on my aunts to accept her offer - I further thought it would [underline]look well[/underline] for [insertion]M[superscript]r[/superscript]. [/insertion]M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. Sneyd that she sh[superscript]d[/superscript]. stay - for I know much has been said by that World which perhaps is not worth caring for but for which they [underline]do[/underline] care. M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. Sneyd in a letter to Honora distinctly says "[underline]she can have no objection to her[/underline] [underline]staying with her aunts[/underline]" In a previous letter to me this indeed was limitted by the words [underline]while we[/underline] [underline]are absent[/underline]. But as the limitation was omitted or withdrawn in M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. S's letter to Ho. she acted upon the [underline]letter[/underline] [underline]of the law[/underline]. We are all gasping with im- patience to know what she will an [Incorrect line]
say in reply to the letter [deletion]sent on monday[/deletion] announcing Honora's [LibNote]210[/LibNote] & my aunts determination that she sh[superscript]d[/superscript]. stay. I am clear M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. Sneyd has no more expectation of it than of the Persian ambassador's being here. We depend upon Francis Beaufort for bringing her back to Ireland about August & perhaps bringing little Francis with them. Francis B. had promised that at any time he w[superscript]ld[/superscript]. undertake her convoy & therefore begged that the difficulty of transport might be no obstacle to any wish she might have to stay in England. - You may know by your own feelings what an agony I shall be in till I hear from you again & find that you approve- - I have promised her that if M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. Sneyds answer does not come [deletion]to day[/deletion][insertion]till after I am gone[/insertion] & that if it does not [underline]accept properly[/underline] I will return for her [deletion]at her[/deletion] or meet her at some appointed place - I will do any anything in short that is most for her happiness - [deletion]I know[/deletion] I personally sacrifice a great deal in giving up her company on my return home - especially in her sympathy & assistance in finishing that manu- script every bit & line of which she has gone over with me & knows better than any one living. I know well too how much Fanny gives up - And what a groaning poor [damage]Ha[unclear]rri[/unclear]et[/damage] will make when she hears Honora is not [damage]coming[/coming] - I see her raging about the room. But still if it is for Honora's happiness & for the happiness of these two dear excellent aunts surely you will all be satisfied. Poor Lucy!- I have never got the image of that poor creatures sufferings out of my head since I read [insertion]last night[/insertion] your des cription of them : How fortunate it was that Gardiner was [deletion]there [/deletion][insertion]with you[/insertion] & that you consulted him : . There is hope that being taken in time all may be set to rights. - & without injury to her shape . . I long to be with you more than pen or words can express.- Lady Granards note and offer was truly kind - Thank God, Fanny is well. There is that comfort for you, that delight in life . She is in much better health and looks that when she came to England and her mind is certainly satisfied & at ease - And you will have her dear mother very soon - [underline]How[/underline] grateful I am to you for all the pleasure & pride I have felt in her - perhaps you will never know because I can never fully say - Your letter about India is admirable - because
it entirely agrees in my own opinion - -both [deletion]about[/deletion] [insertion]as to[/insertion] [underline]India[/underline] & [deletion]about[/deletion] the acceptance of pecuniary favor - I may venture to say this now; as I see how you will decide - Lovells simile - [underline]accepting the ladder[/underline] [underline]& refusing the rungs is very witty & apposite.[/underline] I w[superscript]d[/superscript]. there -fore refuse ladder & rungs - upon the very reasons you & Lovell have stated - and all which my father would have stated in the same circumstances - I will write to Lord C your thanks in your own words - and beg things may remain as they are - What you say about W. & the tenaciousness he would feel about his guardianship had occurred to me - and is of consequence - The sense of dependance in dear little Francis's own mind too would be a strong objection - in short - [insertion]as[/insertion] there is no change to be made in the education imme diately you are right to leave things as they are - but I have no hesitation in deciding my own opinion that the offer should [underline]not[/underline] be accepted for Francis. - Taking in all his chances which Lovell so generously states - But which I sincerely hope [insertion]may be put off by[/insertion] Lovells marrying & having a representative of his own - worthy of him, as he is of his father. Tell that dear brother this with my most affectionate love & high esteem - and now my eyeballs ache so I must have done. Thank you dearest friend for being whether in grief or joy ever the same kindest of friends & mothers to your grateful daughter in law Thank God! Maria Edgeworth My aunts are wonderfully well & much the better I think for having much more to do [deletion]&[/deletion] more motive for exertion & more sense of being of use & [underline]substantive use[/underline]
1819 [deletion]no hesitation in deciding[/deletion] M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. Edgeworth.
